persistence_description: Share of named-party voters who stayed with the same party across two consecutive elections. Isolates party loyalty from turnout volatility.
persistence_stay_rate_hint: Share of voters who chose the same named party in both elections, out of those who voted for an identifiable party in both.
persistence_churn_hint: Share of named-party voters who switched parties between the two elections.
persistence_top_defection_hint: The single largest party-to-party switch — source party, destination, and share of the source party's outflow.
persistence_tap_region_hint: Tap a region on the map to see its details.
persistence_methodology_body: Counts voters whose named-party choice persisted across cycles. Excludes abstainers, newly registered voters, and the small-parties bucket — those would conflate turnout swings with party loyalty.
persistence_footer: Persistence is estimated from per-section voter flow (NNLS Goodman regression + RAS scaling). The party-loyalty signal is robust at the regional level but is an aggregate estimate, not an individual-voter measurement.
benford_description: First-digit and second-digit distributions of per-section vote counts, by party. Compares observed digit shares against the Benford-expected curve.
benford_caveat_title: This is not evidence of fraud
benford_caveat_body: Section-level vote counts are range-bounded, which violates one of Benford's preconditions (numbers spanning multiple orders of magnitude). Plenty of clean electoral data fails the 1st-digit test. Mebane's election-forensics literature explicitly recommends the second-digit test (2BL) over the first-digit test for vote counts. Treat any deviation here as a curiosity prompting further inspection, not a verdict.
benford_mode_first_hint: Classical Benford test on the leading digit. Range-bounded data often fails this test.
benford_mode_second_hint: Mebane's recommended test for election vote counts. Requires sections with at least 10 votes for the party (so the number has a second digit).
benford_mad_caption: Mean absolute deviation in share space — lower is closer to Benford.
benford_chi2_caption: Chi-squared statistic — scales with n, so compare with caution across parties.
benford_p_caption: Approximate upper-tail p-value; values near 0 simply mean "statistically different from Benford", not "fraudulent".
benford_interpretation: How to read this chart: bars are the share of sections whose vote count for this party starts (or has as second digit) with each digit; the line is the Benford-expected share for that digit. A flat bar pattern or a heavy lean toward 1–2 typically signals that the party's per-section counts are small and range-bounded, not that anything is wrong.
benford_footer: Section-level vote counts per party. Sections below the minimum vote threshold are excluded — they cannot inform either test.
benford_bucket_moderate: Moderate deviation — common for parties with mid-size per-section counts.
benford_bucket_strong: Pronounced deviation — usually a sign of small or range-bounded per-section counts, not fraud.
benford_methodology_description: Why this test exists, what its results actually tell us, and when deviations should not mislead us.
benford_methodology_intro: Benford's law describes a surprising regularity: in many naturally-occurring datasets, the leading digit doesn't appear with equal frequency. The digit '1' shows up in about 30% of values, '2' in 18%, and '9' in only 4–5%. The phenomenon appears in city populations, river lengths, accounting ledgers — anywhere numbers span several orders of magnitude. Election forensics borrowed it on the hypothesis that fabricated results would struggle to follow the same curve. Reality is more complicated, and this page is devoted to those nuances.
benford_methodology_h_2bl: Why the test is for the second digit, not the first
benford_methodology_p_2bl1: The classical Benford test analyses the leading (first) digit. It applies when values vary considerably — for example single-digit numbers mixed with three- and four-digit ones. Per-section results don't behave that way. A standard Bulgarian polling station has 800–1000 registered voters. A party with 5–15% support collects votes that vary in a narrow range. That alone is enough for the first-digit test to 'fail' with no hint of manipulation anywhere.
benford_methodology_p_2bl2: Walter Mebane, one of the leading experts on the topic, demonstrates that the second digit is distributed much more uniformly and depends less on the magnitude of the number. The expected second-digit distribution (the '2BL' test) is nearly flat — '0' appears in about 12% of cases, '9' in about 8.5%. This method works on numbers that don't span several orders of magnitude. For that reason the site defaults to 2BL, while the classical first-digit test remains available via a toggle.
benford_methodology_p_thresholds1: We apply two main filters: a minimum number of votes for a party in a given section, and a minimum number of sections that pass that threshold. The second-digit test needs a number with at least two digits — under 10 votes there is simply no second digit to measure. Beyond that, the literature does not impose a high threshold for the votes per section, since the point of 2BL is precisely to work with smaller values. So our threshold is fixed at 10 votes per section, the same for both tests.
benford_methodology_p_thresholds2: Before any result is computed, we require at least 30 sections that pass the threshold. For smaller samples the chi-squared coefficient becomes unstable and MAD becomes too 'noisy'. Parties below this threshold are not displayed, to avoid misleading conclusions.
benford_methodology_h_metrics: How to read MAD and χ²
benford_methodology_p_metrics1: Two values appear under each chart:
benford_methodology_p_metrics_mad: MAD (mean absolute deviation): the average difference between the observed and Benford-expected shares. It does not depend on the number of sections, which makes it suitable for comparison across parties.
benford_methodology_p_metrics_chi: χ² (chi-squared): this value grows proportionally to n (the number of sections). Larger parties will have a higher χ² even if their distribution is identical to a smaller party's.
benford_methodology_p_metrics2: Based on the MAD value, we sort the results into three buckets:
benford_methodology_p_metrics_close: Close to Benford (MAD < 0.04) — the distribution tracks the curve within statistical noise.
benford_methodology_p_metrics_moderate: Moderate deviation (0.04 ≤ MAD < 0.08) — visible difference, often seen in parties whose result is concentrated in a narrow range.
benford_methodology_p_metrics_strong: Strong deviation (MAD ≥ 0.08) — pronounced difference, almost always driven by a specific scale of the data rather than misconduct.
benford_methodology_h_meaning: What the test shows and what it does NOT show
benford_methodology_p_meaning1: Strong deviation from Benford is almost never proof of manipulation. Most often it is an indication of a party with geographically concentrated support, or of results that cluster around specific values. In either case, 'clean' data can produce a bad Benford result.
benford_methodology_p_meaning2: The reverse holds too: a perfect Benford distribution is no certificate of honesty. Manipulation aimed at only a handful of sections, or one that scales the numbers by a constant factor, will not change the curve. Benford is a 'coarse filter' — useful for noticing that one party's data structurally differs from the rest, but not sufficient for final conclusions.
benford_methodology_h_alt: Where to look for real signals
benford_methodology_p_alt1: Three other tools on the site provide much more reliable signals than Benford:
benford_methodology_p_alt_risk: Risk screening: combines turnout outliers, unusual share concentration, machine-vs-paper divergence, and shifts during protocol re-entry.
benford_methodology_p_alt_problem: Problem sections: focuses on sections in higher-risk areas where one party's result departs sharply from the surrounding sections' average.
benford_methodology_p_alt_suspicious: Suspicious sections: sections with large gaps between machine and paper voting, or where final numbers differ substantially from the preliminary count.
benford_methodology_p_refs_mebane: Mebane, W. R. (2006). Election Forensics: Vote Counts and Benford's Law. Working paper, University of Michigan.
benford_methodology_p_refs_pericchi: Pericchi, L. & Torres, D. (2011). Quick Anomaly Detection by the Newcomb–Benford Law. Statistical Science, 26(4), 502–516.
benford_methodology_p_refs_klimek: Klimek, P., Yegorov, Y., Hanel, R. & Thurner, S. (2012). Statistical detection of systematic election irregularities. PNAS, 109(41), 16469–16473.
benford_methodology_p_refs_beber: Beber, B. & Scacco, A. (2012). What the Numbers Say: A Digit-Based Test for Election Fraud. Political Analysis, 20(2), 211–234.
benford_methodology_p_refs_nigrini: Nigrini, M. (2012). Benford's Law: Applications for Forensic Accounting, Auditing, and Fraud Detection. Wiley.
risk_score_description: Composite 0–100 score per polling section, combining seven independent signals already published as standalone reports: recount delta, flash-memory mismatch, invalid-ballot share, additional voters, single-party concentration, statistical outlier vs. settlement peers, and shift vs. the previous election.
risk_score_caveat_title: This is a screening tool, not a fraud determination
risk_score_caveat_body: A high score means the section is statistically unusual along multiple dimensions and warrants a closer look — that is all. Each underlying signal has innocent explanations (small section size, demographic homogeneity, late voter registration drives, recount procedure correctly catching errors). Always check the per-section decomposition before drawing conclusions.
risk_methodology_description: How the risk score is computed: signals, weights, handling of missing data, categories, and how to interpret results.
risk_methodology_caveat_title: This is an analysis tool, not a verdict
risk_methodology_intro: This page describes the methodology for computing the risk score and — more importantly — what it cannot prove. The tool uses data we already publish in our individual reports. Combining them into a single index is a means of prioritizing review, not new evidence of wrongdoing.
risk_methodology_p_what1: Each polling section receives an index from 0 to 100 that summarizes how statistically atypical it is across seven independent criteria. The output is a ranked list of sections, prioritized for review.
risk_methodology_p_what2: It is not a fraud detector, nor a court-admissible irregularity indicator. The system cannot distinguish an administrative error from intentional action — every signal has its possible innocent explanations.
risk_methodology_p_signals_intro: Each signal is a normalized value (0 to 1) drawn from a specific per-section report. If section data for a particular report is missing, the signal stays "undefined" (not zero) and is handled via a special formula.
risk_methodology_signal_recount_p: Measures the magnitude of changes during the official recount: (added + removed votes) / total votes. Captures vote movement even when the net is zero.
risk_methodology_signal_suemg_p: Difference between the machine's flash-memory record (SUEMG) and the final protocol. Can be caused by technical errors during transfer or by recount adjustments not reflected in both records.
risk_methodology_signal_invalid_p: Share of paper ballots ruled invalid. Normalized with a 30% cap (values above that level are taken as 1.0).
risk_methodology_signal_additional_p: Share of voters added "under the line" on election day. Can be a sign of last-minute mobilization in small settlements, institutional voting, or coordinated pressure.
risk_methodology_signal_concentrated_p: Triggers when the winner in the section takes more than 80% of the vote. Below this threshold the signal is 0.
risk_methodology_signal_peer_p: Compares the section's turnout and winner share against neighboring sections in the same settlement (EKATTE). Captures sections that stand out sharply from their immediate surroundings.
risk_methodology_signal_swing_p: Compares the section's turnout and winner share against the same section at the previous election. Captures the "corporate vote" fingerprint — a section that previously split its vote now delivering a lopsided result at elevated turnout. Ratios are compared rather than absolute counts so the signal is robust to section renumbering. Only an upward shift relative to the national norm is flagged; a decline is not.
risk_methodology_p_formula1: The score is a weighted mean, not a simple sum. The denominator includes only the weights of the signals that fired:
risk_methodology_p_formula2: Where n_i is the value of the signal and w_i is its weight. This way, if data for some indicators is missing for a section (e.g. no machine voting), the score is not artificially deflated.
risk_methodology_p_bands_intro: The number is a guide, but in the interface we use four main categories:
risk_methodology_band_low: No notable deviations. Most sections fall here.
risk_methodology_band_elevated: Some signals worth attention, but they can be coincidental.
risk_methodology_band_critical: Highly unusual data across multiple signals. A mandatory protocol review is recommended.
risk_methodology_important_percentile_p: Since high deviations are demographically normal in some municipalities (e.g. small mountain villages), the percentile helps determine whether a score is high only for that municipality or for the country as a whole.
risk_methodology_important_party_p: This column shows the section's winning party — same convention as every other per-section report. It is NOT the party "affected" by the risk. For specific information on which party gained or lost votes during a recount or flash-memory adjustment, see the per-section breakdown.
risk_methodology_important_evidence_p: A score of 85 based on 5 of 7 signals is much more reliable than the same score based on only 2 signals. Always check the count of available signals (e.g. 3/7).
risk_methodology_important_context_p: The badge for specific neighborhoods is informational only and does not affect the mathematical computation of risk. It helps the reader judge whether the demographic factor explains high concentration.
risk_methodology_p_how_to_use1: Start with the category, not the raw number. Check the count of available signals: scores backed by fewer than 3 signals are analysis-grade only and should not be used as evidence individually.
risk_methodology_p_how_to_use2: Open the per-section breakdown before quoting a score. The waterfall shows which signals fired and at what magnitude — without this context the number is meaningless. A score of 75 built from "all seven signals slightly elevated" is a very different story from a score of 75 built from "recount adjustment +35, everything else zero."
risk_percentile_caption: Higher = more unusual relative to peer sections in the same municipality.
risk_partial_signals_hint: Some signals couldn't be computed — score reflects only the available ones.
risk_full_signals_hint: Score uses every signal — most reliable measurement.
risk_neighborhood_flag: Section sits in a flagged Roma neighborhood (context only — not part of the score).
risk_interpretation: Each signal is normalized to 0–1, multiplied by its weight, then summed. Missing signals are excluded from both numerator and denominator so a thin-data score isn't artificially deflated — but watch the signals-available count: a high score from 2 of 7 signals is far less reliable than a high score from 5 of 7.
risk_footer: Composite of seven independent signals — recount, flash-memory, invalid ballots, additional voters, concentration, peer outlier, shift vs. previous election.
risk_score_not_found: Section not found in the risk-screening report for this election.

about_p_1: Between 2021 and 2026, Bulgaria held an unprecedented seven parliamentary elections without producing a stable, lasting government. The political churn shaped public life and put the country's reforms on hold.
about_p_2: The snap election of 19 April 2026 broke that cycle. A clear parliamentary majority emerged, a new government was sworn in, and for the first time in years a full term in office looks plausible. Whether that stability holds — and whether it translates into policy — is the open question of this political moment.
about_p_3: The years of churn had a real cost. Voter fatigue and disillusionment with politicians created an environment where radical voices, aided by Moscow's disinformation campaigns, gained ground at the expense of the democratic process. Bulgaria slipped behind on EU recovery funds, on the eurozone timeline, and on Schengen integration.
about_p_4: Bulgaria also became fertile ground for conspiracy theories and growing distrust in the electoral system. This platform exists to counteract that — by providing transparent, granular access to real election data and offering an intuitive way to analyze the results.
project_about: This project began as a collaborative effort between a father and son, designed to be open-source and welcoming to contributors from all backgrounds. With a focus on versatility and ease of use, it provides access to comprehensive election results from the past 10 years, empowering everyone to explore and engage with the data.

mp_profiles_source: Photos and biographies of National Assembly members
rollcall_votes_source: Roll-call vote CSVs from stenogram attachments (powers loyalty, attendance, group cohesion, and the voting-twins analytics)
mp_declarations_source: Property and interest declarations from the Court of Audit (register.cacbg.bg) — MPs, cabinet members, deputy ministers, state-agency heads, regional governors, mayors and municipal councillors
bgpost_postcodes_source: Bulgarian Post postal-code register (data.egov.bg, CC0) — joined to the NSI settlements catalog to resolve the free-text 'registered office' field on cacbg.bg declarations to a specific settlement, so the 'Companies HQ'd here' tile on each settlement page can list MP-linked firms by their headquarters address. Sofia city (postal code 1000…1799) collapses to a single capital entry — no rayon split, as no public dataset cleanly maps Sofia postcodes to the 24 administrative rayons.
local_government_roster_source: Roster of mayors, deputy mayors, council chairs, councillors and chief architects per municipality — derived from the Court of Audit declarations and surfaced on every settlement page
local_taxes_ipi_source: Institute for Market Economics — 265 municipalities: annual FOI-collected survey of municipal tax rates. Covers property tax (legal entities), property-transfer tax, vehicle tax (74–110 kW), retail patent tax (≤100 m²) and taxi patent tax — all 265 municipalities, 2021–2025. Powers the "Local taxes" tile on My Area.
local_taxes_regional_profiles_source: regionalprofiles.bg — analytical companion to the IME dataset, with interactive maps and 2012–2026 comparisons.
local_taxes_naredba_source: Per-municipality bylaws on local taxes and fees (the obshtini.bg platform hosts ~150 of them; the rest publish PDFs on their own sites). Source for residential garbage fee with its basis flag (promille / users / floor area / waste volume), property tax for individuals (where the TAX bylaw is reachable), tourist tax and dog tax — slots IME does not cover.
council_minutes_source: Council resolutions, aggregate vote tallies (за/против/въздържал) and where available the per-councillor named-vote breakdown — scraped from each municipality's own общински-съвет website (protocol PDFs and per-decision extracts). Bulgaria has no central register, so this fans out across heterogeneous CMSes per município. Sixteen municipalities are wired: Sofia, Plovdiv, Varna, Burgas, Stara Zagora, Pleven, Ruse, Велико Търново, Sliven, Габрово, Казанлък, Хасково, Добрич, Димитровград, Разград, and Перник (Габрово, Казанлък, Хасково and Добрич added via Wayback CDX discovery; Димитровград, Разград, and Перник added from clean live WordPress / Joomla indexes — Перник is a tier-A per-councillor source with 28 councillors over 158 resolutions joined at ~82% to the cacbg roster); Blagoevgrad is deferred (legacy URL dead, município migrated to a SaaS that requires auth). Per-councillor named-vote breakdowns are live for Велико Търново (~81%), Burgas (~94% across 86 sessions, pulled from a parallel /video archive that links the full session protokol PDF) and Sofia (~89% across 75 sessions, recovered via Gemini Vision OCR of the full-protocol PDFs since they ship as Cyrillic-to-Latin mojibake). These surface in the My-Area "How the council voted" tile with one mini-avatar per councillor coloured by vote. Tally + adopted/rejected ship for Велико Търново, Stara Zagora, Ruse, Pleven, Burgas and Sofia; the others (Plovdiv, Varna, Sliven) publish in ПРЕПИС-ИЗВЛЕЧЕНИЕ format which carries decision text but no vote counts.

polls_description: How accurate have the major Bulgarian polling agencies been? Comparing their final pre-election polls against the actual results from the Central Election Commission, with party-level bias and ideological-bloc lean.
polls_leaderboard_hint: Mean absolute error in percentage points across each agency's final pre-election polls. Lower is better.
polls_election_accuracy_hint: Each agency's last poll before the election, compared to the actual result.
polls_data_source: Source: BG Wikipedia polling tables · Actual results: Central Election Commission · Analysis: Claude Opus 4.7
polls_no_data_for_election: No agency polling data on file for the {{date}} election. The cross-election analysis below still applies.
polls_no_polls_for_agency: No polls on file for this agency yet.
polls_no_detail_breakdown: No party breakdown on file for this poll.
polls_actual_marker_legend: The dark vertical line on each bar marks the actual election result.
polls_section_all_time_hint: Aggregated across every election cycle in our dataset, not just the one selected above.
dashboard_polls_hint: Each agency's final pre-election poll vs. the actual result for this election. MAE = mean absolute error in percentage points across parties.
dashboard_polls_mae_hint: Mean absolute error of this agency's final pre-election poll for this election, in percentage points across parties. Lower is better. Differs from the corrected MAE on the polls page, which pools every election and applies Bayesian shrinkage.
dashboard_polls_days_before_hint: Days between the poll's fieldwork end and election day. Polls farther from the vote tend to score worse — that's a recency artefact, not a methodological one.
dashboard_polls_biggest_miss_hint: The party where this agency's final poll was most off. Signed difference vs. the actual result in percentage points — positive means the agency overestimated, negative means it underestimated.
dashboard_accuracy_trends_hint: Cross-agency average mean absolute error (MAE) per election. Bars above the dashed line are elections where polls were collectively further off than usual — often a signal that something unexpected happened (a late shift, a new party, or a sampling miss).
polls_anomalous_hint: When every agency misses by more than 3pp, it usually points to a late shift in voter sentiment or a party that was hard to sample.
polls_methodology_intro: Each agency's score is based on the mean absolute error (MAE) in percentage points across named parties. It is computed by comparing the agency's last pre-election poll to the official CEC result — the smaller the error, the more accurate the poll.
polls_methodology_section1_body: Polls that explicitly publish an undecided share are normalised before scoring. The undecided share is redistributed proportionally across the named parties so the sum matches the official result's denominator (based only on those who voted).
polls_methodology_section1_icon_note: A scale icon next to an MAE value indicates that proportional redistribution was applied to that poll.
polls_methodology_section1_genre_note: If the agency publishes a finished forecast (rather than raw attitudes), the values are scored as-is to avoid double-counting the undecided share.
polls_methodology_section2_body: To prevent a single lucky cycle from topping the table, the leaderboard uses corrected MAE. An agency's per-election errors are pooled across every election it covered and pulled toward the cross-agency mean via Bayesian shrinkage.
polls_methodology_section2_formula: (n × agency_MAE + k × overall_mean_MAE) / (n + k), with n = elections covered and k = 4
polls_methodology_section2_logic_note: An agency with 1 election weights only 1/5 to its own number and 4/5 to the cross-agency mean. An 8-cycle agency weights 8/12 to itself and 4/12 to the mean.
polls_methodology_section3_body: The final grade is a composite score based on three components:
polls_methodology_section3_signal2: Plus/Minus vs. consensus: rewards agencies (up to ±0.5) that successfully forecast deviations from the cross-agency trend.
polls_methodology_section3_signal3: 4%-barrier accuracy: rewards correct over/under-threshold placements via a 1.5× multiplier.
polls_methodology_caveat: Polls farther from the vote (larger "days before") tend to score worse. That's typically a recency artefact (sentiment shifts in the final days), not necessarily a methodological flaw in the agency itself.
polls_normalization_applied: Normalisation applied: {{pp}}pp of undecided share redistributed across the named parties before scoring.
polls_median_days_before_hint: Median number of days between fieldwork end and election day across this agency's scored polls. Lower means the agency typically polls right before the vote — a structural accuracy advantage. A high number signals stale polls dragging the score down.
polls_grade_hint: Composite letter grade A+ through F derived from shrunk MAE, Plus/Minus, and barrier-call accuracy. Tunable thresholds; see methodology.
polls_shrunk_mae_hint: Sample-weighted, industry-bias-subtracted MAE put through Bayesian shrinkage. For each (election, party) the cross-agency mean error is subtracted from every agency's error before |·| — so cycle-wide forecast shocks (a new party absorbing late-deciders, an industry-wide turnout-model miss) aren't charged to individual agencies. Each per-poll contribution is then weighted by √n. Same Bayesian shrinkage as before applies on top: (n × MAE_adj + k × overall_mean) / (n + k), k=4. Reads as 'skill relative to peers', not absolute error.
polls_plus_minus_hint: Average difference between this agency's MAE and the consensus MAE of all other agencies on the same election. Positive means the agency consistently beat the field; negative means it consistently lagged.
polls_barrier_call_hint: Share of (party, election) pairs where the agency's last poll correctly placed the party above or below the 4% parliamentary barrier.

vote_flow_no_data: No flow estimate available for this cycle pair.
vote_flow_methodology_description: How the "where the votes went" estimates are computed and what they can and cannot tell you.
vote_flow_methodology_intro: Election results tell us how many votes each party received in each section, but not which voters moved where. The Sankey chart reconstructs flows from observed margins using ecological regression — a statistical inference, not a measurement of individuals.
vote_flow_methodology_p_estimate1: For each oblast, we run a Goodman-style non-negative least-squares regression across the oblast's polling sections. Each section contributes one equation per to-cycle party, fit against the from-cycle party shares as predictors. The fitted coefficients form a transition matrix: the share of party A's voters in cycle T who voted for party B in cycle T+1. Sections are weighted by registered voter count.
vote_flow_methodology_p_estimate2: We then biproportionally scale the matrix (the RAS algorithm) so that each row exactly sums to that party's published from-cycle vote total and each column exactly sums to its to-cycle total. Per-oblast matrices are summed in absolute votes to produce the national view.
vote_flow_methodology_p_pseudo1: Some Sankey nodes don't represent parties — they represent the rest of the electorate so the chart conserves the registered-voter pool:
vote_flow_methodology_p_pseudo_abstain: "Did not vote" — registered voters who didn't cast a valid party vote (includes invalid ballots and "I support no one" votes).
vote_flow_methodology_p_pseudo_joined: "Newly registered" — appears on the from side when the rolls grew between cycles. Represents people who became eligible (turned 18, returned from abroad) before the second election.
vote_flow_methodology_p_pseudo_exited: "Removed from rolls" — appears on the to side when the rolls shrank. Represents people who left the rolls (death, deregistration) after the first election.
vote_flow_methodology_p_pseudo_small: "Other parties" — combined bucket for parties that scored below 1% in both cycles, to keep the chart legible.
vote_flow_methodology_h_scope: Why only at oblast level and above
vote_flow_methodology_p_scope: The estimate works because sections within an oblast voted differently from one another — those differences are exactly what lets the regression separate out "what fraction of party A's voters went to party B". At finer scopes there simply aren't enough differences to learn from: a typical municipality has 30–50 sections, a settlement often only 1–3, and a single section has none. The math becomes underdetermined — many different transition stories fit the observed totals equally well. That's why on municipality, settlement and section pages we show the raw before-after deltas (Party X: 32% → 28%) instead of inventing a flow estimate.
vote_flow_methodology_p_caveat_coalition: Coalition splits and mergers (PP+DB merger, ITN's collapse, the DPS split into ДПС-НН and АПС) appear as cross-party flows even when they reflect administrative changes more than individual voter movement. Treat such transitions with extra skepticism.
vote_flow_methodology_p_caveat_sections: We match polling sections by ID. About 1–3% of sections per cycle pair have unstable IDs (re-issued, mobile sections, ship sections); those are dropped from the regression and reported as "Sections dropped" in the diagnostics.
vote_flow_methodology_p_caveat_abroad: Abroad sections (oblast 32) are excluded entirely — their section IDs are re-issued every cycle, so a section-level join produces noise rather than signal.
vote_flow_methodology_p_caveat_oblast: Oblast-level estimates have higher uncertainty than the national rollup, especially in small oblasts and on cycle pairs with brand-new parties. The diagnostic residual on each cycle pair gives a sense of how cleanly the math balanced.
vote_flow_methodology_p_trust1: The biggest flows on stable cycle pairs (party self-loops, abstain-to-abstain, well-known migrations between similar parties) are robust — they come out the same under multiple model variants. Small flows between similar-sized parties are noisy and should be read as a rough order of magnitude rather than a precise count.
vote_flow_methodology_p_trust2: Activation and demobilisation flows (party ↔ "Did not vote") are usually the most informative ones, because Bulgarian turnout has swung enormously between cycles. Most of what looks like "voters defecting between parties" is in fact activation/demobilisation in disguise.

candidate_namesake_intro: Multiple candidates share this name. Pick the one you meant:
source_declarations: Source: property and interest declarations filed with the Bulgarian Court of Audit (Сметна палата). Sitting MPs cannot legally hold management roles, so this list covers ownership stakes only.
dashboard_region_mps_hint: MPs elected from this region for the corresponding National Assembly. The party shown is the one each candidate ran with. Photos and biographies are from parliament.bg. Hover a dot or avatar for details.
dashboard_party_voters_hint: Total votes cast for this party in this election, with delta vs the prior election. The percentage is the party's share of all valid votes nationally.
dashboard_party_position_hint: The party's overall ranking by total votes in this election, with arrow showing change vs the prior election.
dashboard_party_paper_machine_hint: How this party's voters split between paper ballots and voting machines, with the change vs prior election. Useful for spotting demographic shifts in the party's base.
dashboard_party_top_region_hint: The region contributing the most votes to this party. Position is the party's rank within that region's vote.
dashboard_party_top_regions_hint: All electoral regions ranked by votes for this party. Bar = absolute votes; % = party's share of the region's vote; change = percentage-point swing vs prior election. Click a region to drill into its full results.
dashboard_party_top_municipalities_hint: Municipalities where this party drew the most votes. % local = party's share of that municipality's vote; change = absolute vote difference vs prior election.
dashboard_party_top_settlements_hint: Towns and villages where this party drew the most votes. % local = party's share of that settlement's vote; change = absolute vote difference vs prior election.
dashboard_party_swings_hint: Regions where the party gained or lost the most percentage points vs the prior election. Useful for identifying where to defend, attack, or investigate.
dashboard_party_fingerprint_hint: How this party's vote share correlates with each demographic across all 265 municipalities (Pearson r, +1 = perfect alignment, −1 = perfect inverse). Click any row to open the cross-tab scatter for that pair.
dashboard_party_fingerprint_note: Pearson correlation across {{count}} demographic dimensions, computed over all 265 municipalities.
dashboard_demographic_cleavages_hint: How the top parties' municipal vote shares correlate with each demographic dimension across all 265 municipalities. Wider spread between dots = the dimension was a sharper dividing line in this election. Click any row for the cross-tab scatter.
dashboard_demographic_cleavages_note: Each dot is one party that cleared the 4% threshold; horizontal position is the Pearson r between that party's municipal vote share and the demographic.
dashboard_party_assessment_hint: AI-written analysis of this party's performance — what worked, what didn't, and where to focus next campaign. Generated offline from the party's vote, polling, and (where available) financing data.
dashboard_assessment_not_generated: Retrospect not yet generated for this party. Run the party-retrospect skill to produce one.
dashboard_party_top_candidates_hint: The party's strongest preferential-vote candidates — those whose personal vote count was highest within the party. Click a candidate for the full profile across regions.
dashboard_party_raised_funds_hint: Total funds raised by this party for the campaign — sum of contributions from the party itself, donors, candidates, and the state media package. Delta compares to the same party's total in the prior election.
dashboard_party_campaign_cost_hint: Total declared campaign expenses including non-monetary contributions. A higher cost than the prior cycle is shown in red, lower in green. The secondary line shows how much of the cost came from non-monetary contributions.
dashboard_party_top_expense_hint: The single largest expense bucket on this party's filing — useful as a quick read of where the campaign budget went.
dashboard_party_donors_count_hint: Number of declared individual donors and the total amount they contributed. The candidates line shows the same for candidate self-funding. Click the count to see the full donor list.
dashboard_party_expense_breakdown_hint: All campaign expense categories ranked by amount. Bar = absolute spend; share = percent of total cost; change = percent change vs the prior election (red = increase, green = decrease).
dashboard_party_top_donors_hint: The 10 largest individual donors to this party's campaign. Bar = donor's amount relative to the largest donor; share = percent of all donor contributions.
dashboard_candidate_preferences_hint: Total preferential votes the candidate received across all regions, with the percent change vs the prior election. The secondary share is the candidate's slice of the party's total preferences this election.
dashboard_candidate_paper_machine_hint: How the candidate's preferential votes split between paper ballots and voting machines, with the change vs prior election.
dashboard_candidate_ballot_hint: List position(s) the candidate occupied on the party ballot. Some candidates ran in more than one region; each region has its own list number.
dashboard_candidate_top_region_hint: The region where the candidate drew the most preferential votes. Share is relative to all preferential votes for the party in that region.
dashboard_candidate_regions_hint: Per-region breakdown of preferential votes for this candidate. Bar = absolute votes; % region = candidate's share of all valid votes in that region; change = absolute vote difference vs prior election.
dashboard_candidate_top_settlements_hint: Towns and villages where the candidate drew the most preferential votes. % of party = candidate's share of the party's preferences in that settlement; % local = candidate's share of all valid votes in that settlement.
dashboard_candidate_top_sections_hint: Polling stations where the candidate drew the most preferential votes. % of party = candidate's share of the party's preferences in that station; % local = candidate's share of all valid votes in that station.
dashboard_candidate_trajectory_hint: Preferential votes for this candidate across consecutive elections, broken down by region. Useful for spotting whether the candidate's personal vote is growing or fading.
dashboard_candidate_donations_hint: Self-funded contributions declared by this candidate to their party's campaign. Total combines monetary and non-monetary contributions.

Source: register.cacbg.bg (Bulgarian Court of Audit). Scope: cabinet, state-agency heads, and regional governors. Mayors and judiciary are tracked separately.
official_not_found_desc: No declarations on file under this slug. The official may be from a category we don't ingest, or the URL is wrong.
official_connections_note: Companies this official is tied to via declared stakes or the Commerce Registry, and the MPs and officials who share those companies. Name-only Commerce Registry matches are not corroborated.

cohesion_description: How unified Bulgarian parliamentary groups vote — mean and median cohesion per item across the current parliament.
cohesion_intro: How unified each parliamentary group is when its members vote. Cohesion = share of the group voting the same way per item, averaged across every item the group participated in. 1.00 means every member voted the same way every time; 0.50 is an even split. Absences are excluded.
